[[Iōno clan]] (samurai)
[[Inazawa clan#Nasu Inazawa clan|Inazawa clan]] (samurai) }} The '''Nasu clan''' (那須氏) was a [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] and [[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] family from [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. They originated from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]'s [[:en:Nasu District|Nasu District]] and became widely known for their member [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], who famously shot a fan off a ship during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]] while fighting for [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] in the [[:en:Genpei War|Genpei War]].{{Kotobank|1=Nasu clan|2=World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition}} Following the establishment of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], they unified under the Nasu clan head in [[:en:Karasuyama, Tochigi|Karasuyama]] and led the [[Nasu warriors]] until the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. After being dispossessed during [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s conquest of [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]], they regained limited status following the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], becoming the [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[Nasu Domain]] and later the [[:en:Karasuyama Domain|Karasuyama Domain]] until their confiscation in 1687 (Jōkyō 4).{{Kotobank|1=Nasu Domain|2=Nipponica}} The family retained their name as [[kōtaiyoriai]] (rotational guards) and joined the [[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] Rank after the [[Meiji Restoration]].{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Origins == The Nasu clan claimed descent from the [[:en:Fujiwara Hokke|Fujiwara Hokke]] through [[Fujiwara no Nagai]]'s lineage, with the purported founder being his grandson [[Fujiwara no Sukie|Sukie]] (also known as Sadafumi).{{Efn|Research suggests that Sukie’s father, [[Fujiwara no Michie (Mikosazuke branch)|Fujiwara no Michie]], may have died young, making Sukie’s lineage questionable.Takako Noguchi, "Residential Inheritance in Heian Aristocracy," in {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Takako Noguchi|title=Space and Time of Heian Aristocracy|publisher=Seibundo Shuppan|date=2024|isbn=978-4-7924-1533-4|pages=97-99}}}} Initially called the Sudo clan, they adopted the Nasu name during the era of [[Nasu Suketaka]]."Nasu Genealogy" in {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従}} Vol. 149.{{Efn|Some versions of the genealogy, such as "Yamanouchi Sudo Genealogy," attribute their lineage to [[Fujiwara no Tamenori]]'s descendants.}} Although the clan is most famously associated with [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] for his exploits during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]], verifiable historical records begin with [[Nasu Mitsusuke]] in the early [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. Questions surround earlier genealogies and tales, with some theories suggesting descent from the [[Nasu no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko]] family, an ancient local ruling family.Ryo Ota, "Nasu, surname of Fujiwara," in {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Ryo Ota|title=Surname and Family Dictionary|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1942}}. == Overview == After the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] was established, the Nasu clan served as [[:en:Gokenin|Gokenin]] (direct vassals). They reached their zenith during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], being counted among the prestigious [[Kantō Eight Manors]]. However, internal strife in the 15th century divided the clan into the Upper and Lower Nasu families, weakening their position.{{Efn|Evidence suggests this division began earlier, during the [[Nanboku-chō period]].{{Sfn|Eda|2008|p={{page needed|date=2023}}}}}} The Upper Nasu aligned with the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]], while the Lower Nasu supported the [[:en:Kamakura Kubō|Kamakura Kubō]] and later the [[Koga Kubō]]. Despite briefly reunifying under [[Nasu Sukefusa]] in 1514 (Eishō 11), the clan's influence waned due to ongoing conflicts with neighboring [[Utsunomiya clan|Utsunomiya]] and [[:en:Satake clan|Satake clans]]. During [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s [[:en:Odawara Campaign|Odawara Campaign]], [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]]'s delayed arrival led to their dispossession, though his son [[Nasu Sukekage]] retained 5,000 koku under the mediation of [[Ōtawara Harukiyo]]. After siding with the [[Tokugawa clan]] during the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], the clan was granted 14,000 koku and became the daimyō of the Nasu Domain, later moving to the Karasuyama Domain with 20,000 koku. The domain was confiscated in 1687 due to the [[Karasuyama Incident]], leaving the clan with 1,000 koku as kōtaiyoriai under [[Nasu Sukemasa]]. The last head before the [[Meiji Restoration]], [[Nasu Suketaka]], supported the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]], receiving courtier status and participating in the [[Upper Council]] as a representative.{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Post-war period == After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the Nasu family lost most of its land holdings due to [[:en:Land reform|Land reform]]. The family relocated to [[:en:Hirosaki|Hirosaki]], [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], seeking aid from their relatives in the [[:en:Tsugaru clan|Tsugaru clan]]. The 36th head, [[Nasu Takashi]], became embroiled in the [[Hirosaki University Professor's Wife Murder Case]], later exonerated after 30 years.{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Nasu Takashi|title=Interview: The Agony of Wrongful Conviction|journal=[[Law Seminar]]|volume=37|issue=1|pages=84-88|year=1992}} In 2004, Takashi entrusted 701 heirlooms, including items linked to [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], to the city of [[:en:Ōtawara|Ōtawara]], helping establish the [[Nasu no Yoichi Denshōkan]] museum.Nasu Yoichi Museum pamphlet, 2004. The 37th head, [[Nasu Masami]], continues to maintain the clan’s legacy. == Key Figures == * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Yoichi) * [[Nasu Sukesada]] (Gorō) * [[Nasu Sukemasa]] (First unified head) * [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]] * [[Nasu Sukekage]] (Founder of Nasu Domain) * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Final daimyō) == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Nasu Seven Cavalry]] * [[Ōtawara clan]] * [[Ōzeki clan]] * [[Nasu Chronicles]] * [[Chisaka clan]] * [[Chisaka Kagechika]] * [[Nasu warriors]] {{translated page|ja|那須氏|version=102902072|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nasu clan]] [[ja:那須氏]] [[uk:%D0%A0%D1%96%D0%B4 %D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%83]] [[de:Nasu (Klan)]] [[it:Clan Nasu]] [[Category:History of Otawara City]] [[Category:History of Yaita City]] [[Category:Nasu Clan]] [[Category:那須烏山の Municipal History]] [[Category:Clans of Kantō Region]] [[Category:Samurai Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] [[uk:Рід Насу]]

[[Iōno clan]] (samurai)
[[Inazawa clan#Nasu Inazawa clan|Inazawa clan]] (samurai) }} The '''Nasu clan''' (那須氏) was a [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] and [[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] family from [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. They originated from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]'s [[:en:Nasu District|Nasu District]] and became widely known for their member [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], who famously shot a fan off a ship during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]] while fighting for [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] in the [[:en:Genpei War|Genpei War]].{{Kotobank|1=Nasu clan|2=World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition}} Following the establishment of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], they unified under the Nasu clan head in [[:en:Karasuyama, Tochigi|Karasuyama]] and led the [[Nasu warriors]] until the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. After being dispossessed during [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s conquest of [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]], they regained limited status following the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], becoming the [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[Nasu Domain]] and later the [[:en:Karasuyama Domain|Karasuyama Domain]] until their confiscation in 1687 (Jōkyō 4).{{Kotobank|1=Nasu Domain|2=Nipponica}} The family retained their name as [[kōtaiyoriai]] (rotational guards) and joined the [[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] Rank after the [[Meiji Restoration]].{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Origins == The Nasu clan claimed descent from the [[:en:Fujiwara Hokke|Fujiwara Hokke]] through [[Fujiwara no Nagai]]'s lineage, with the purported founder being his grandson [[Fujiwara no Sukie|Sukie]] (also known as Sadafumi).{{Efn|Research suggests that Sukie’s father, [[Fujiwara no Michie (Mikosazuke branch)|Fujiwara no Michie]], may have died young, making Sukie’s lineage questionable.Takako Noguchi, "Residential Inheritance in Heian Aristocracy," in {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Takako Noguchi|title=Space and Time of Heian Aristocracy|publisher=Seibundo Shuppan|date=2024|isbn=978-4-7924-1533-4|pages=97-99}}}} Initially called the Sudo clan, they adopted the Nasu name during the era of [[Nasu Suketaka]]."Nasu Genealogy" in {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}} Vol. 149.{{Efn|Some versions of the genealogy, such as "Yamanouchi Sudo Genealogy," attribute their lineage to [[Fujiwara no Tamenori]]'s descendants.}} Although the clan is most famously associated with [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] for his exploits during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]], verifiable historical records begin with [[Nasu Mitsusuke]] in the early [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. Questions surround earlier genealogies and tales, with some theories suggesting descent from the [[Nasu no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko]] family, an ancient local ruling family.Ryo Ota, "Nasu, surname of Fujiwara," in {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Ryo Ota|title=Surname and Family Dictionary|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1942}}. == Overview == After the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] was established, the Nasu clan served as [[:en:Gokenin|Gokenin]] (direct vassals). They reached their zenith during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], being counted among the prestigious [[Kantō Eight Manors]]. However, internal strife in the 15th century divided the clan into the Upper and Lower Nasu families, weakening their position.{{Efn|Evidence suggests this division began earlier, during the [[Nanboku-chō period]].{{Sfn|Eda|2008|p={{page needed|date=2023}}}}}} The Upper Nasu aligned with the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]], while the Lower Nasu supported the [[:en:Kamakura Kubō|Kamakura Kubō]] and later the [[Koga Kubō]]. Despite briefly reunifying under [[Nasu Sukefusa]] in 1514 (Eishō 11), the clan's influence waned due to ongoing conflicts with neighboring [[Utsunomiya clan|Utsunomiya]] and [[:en:Satake clan|Satake clans]]. During [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s [[:en:Odawara Campaign|Odawara Campaign]], [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]]'s delayed arrival led to their dispossession, though his son [[Nasu Sukekage]] retained 5,000 koku under the mediation of [[Ōtawara Harukiyo]]. After siding with the [[Tokugawa clan]] during the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], the clan was granted 14,000 koku and became the daimyō of the Nasu Domain, later moving to the Karasuyama Domain with 20,000 koku. The domain was confiscated in 1687 due to the [[Karasuyama Incident]], leaving the clan with 1,000 koku as kōtaiyoriai under [[Nasu Sukemasa]]. The last head before the [[Meiji Restoration]], [[Nasu Suketaka]], supported the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]], receiving courtier status and participating in the [[Upper Council]] as a representative.{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Post-war period == After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the Nasu family lost most of its land holdings due to [[:en:Land reform|Land reform]]. The family relocated to [[:en:Hirosaki|Hirosaki]], [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], seeking aid from their relatives in the [[:en:Tsugaru clan|Tsugaru clan]]. The 36th head, [[Nasu Takashi]], became embroiled in the [[Hirosaki University Professor's Wife Murder Case]], later exonerated after 30 years.{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Nasu Takashi|title=Interview: The Agony of Wrongful Conviction|journal=[[Law Seminar]]|volume=37|issue=1|pages=84-88|year=1992}} In 2004, Takashi entrusted 701 heirlooms, including items linked to [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], to the city of [[:en:Ōtawara|Ōtawara]], helping establish the [[Nasu no Yoichi Denshōkan]] museum.Nasu Yoichi Museum pamphlet, 2004. The 37th head, [[Nasu Masami]], continues to maintain the clan’s legacy. == Key Figures == * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Yoichi) * [[Nasu Sukesada]] (Gorō) * [[Nasu Sukemasa]] (First unified head) * [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]] * [[Nasu Sukekage]] (Founder of Nasu Domain) * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Final daimyō) == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Nasu Seven Cavalry]] * [[Ōtawara clan]] * [[Ōzeki clan]] * [[Nasu Chronicles]] * [[Chisaka clan]] * [[Chisaka Kagechika]] * [[Nasu warriors]] {{translated page|ja|那須氏|version=102902072|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nasu clan]] [[ja:那須氏]] [[uk:%D0%A0%D1%96%D0%B4 %D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%83]] [[de:Nasu (Klan)]] [[it:Clan Nasu]] [[Category:History of Otawara City]] [[Category:History of Yaita City]] [[Category:Nasu Clan]] [[Category:History of Nasukarasuyama City]] [[Category:Clans of Kantō Region]] [[Category:Samurai Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] [[uk:Рід Насу]]

[[Inazawa clan#Nasu Inazawa clan|Inazawa clan]] (samurai) }} The '''Nasu clan''' (那須氏) was a [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] and [[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] family from [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. They originated from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]'s [[:en:Nasu District|Nasu District]] and became widely known for their member [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], who famously shot a fan off a ship during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]] while fighting for [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] in the [[:en:Genpei War|Genpei War]].{{Kotobank|1=Nasu clan|2=World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition}} Following the establishment of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], they unified under the Nasu clan head in [[:en:Karasuyama, Tochigi|Karasuyama]] and led the [[Nasu warriors]] until the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. After being dispossessed during [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s conquest of [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]], they regained limited status following the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], becoming the [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[Nasu Domain]] and later the [[:en:Karasuyama Domain|Karasuyama Domain]] until their confiscation in 1687 (Jōkyō 4).{{Kotobank|1=Nasu Domain|2=Nipponica}} The family retained their name as [[kōtaiyoriai]] (rotational guards) and joined the [[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] Rank after the [[Meiji Restoration]].{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Origins == The Nasu clan claimed descent from the [[:en:Fujiwara Hokke|Fujiwara Hokke]] through [[Fujiwara no Nagai]]'s lineage, with the purported founder being his grandson [[Fujiwara no Sukie|Sukie]] (also known as Sadafumi).{{Efn|Research suggests that Sukie’s father, [[Fujiwara no Michie (Mikosazuke branch)|Fujiwara no Michie]], may have died young, making Sukie’s lineage questionable.Takako Noguchi, "Residential Inheritance in Heian Aristocracy," in {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Takako Noguchi|title=Space and Time of Heian Aristocracy|publisher=Seibundo Shuppan|date=2024|isbn=978-4-7924-1533-4|pages=97-99}}}} Initially called the Sudo clan, they adopted the Nasu name during the era of [[Nasu Suketaka]]."Nasu Genealogy" in {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}} Vol. 149.{{Efn|Some versions of the genealogy, such as "Yamanouchi Sudo Genealogy," attribute their lineage to [[Fujiwara no Tamenori]]'s descendants.}} Although the clan is most famously associated with [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] for his exploits during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]], verifiable historical records begin with [[Nasu Mitsusuke]] in the early [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. Questions surround earlier genealogies and tales, with some theories suggesting descent from the [[Nasu no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko]] family, an ancient local ruling family.Ryo Ota, "Nasu, surname of Fujiwara," in {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Ryo Ota|title=Surname and Family Dictionary|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1942}}. == Overview == After the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] was established, the Nasu clan served as [[:en:Gokenin|Gokenin]] (direct vassals). They reached their zenith during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], being counted among the prestigious [[Kantō Eight Manors]]. However, internal strife in the 15th century divided the clan into the Upper and Lower Nasu families, weakening their position.{{Efn|Evidence suggests this division began earlier, during the [[Nanboku-chō period]].{{Sfn|Eda|2008|p={{page needed|date=2023}}}}}} The Upper Nasu aligned with the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]], while the Lower Nasu supported the [[:en:Kamakura Kubō|Kamakura Kubō]] and later the [[Koga Kubō]]. Despite briefly reunifying under [[Nasu Sukefusa]] in 1514 (Eishō 11), the clan's influence waned due to ongoing conflicts with neighboring [[Utsunomiya clan|Utsunomiya]] and [[:en:Satake clan|Satake clans]]. During [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s [[:en:Odawara Campaign|Odawara Campaign]], [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]]'s delayed arrival led to their dispossession, though his son [[Nasu Sukekage]] retained 5,000 koku under the mediation of [[Ōtawara Harukiyo]]. After siding with the [[Tokugawa clan]] during the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], the clan was granted 14,000 koku and became the daimyō of the Nasu Domain, later moving to the Karasuyama Domain with 20,000 koku. The domain was confiscated in 1687 due to the [[Karasuyama Incident]], leaving the clan with 1,000 koku as kōtaiyoriai under [[Nasu Sukemasa]]. The last head before the [[Meiji Restoration]], [[Nasu Suketaka]], supported the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]], receiving courtier status and participating in the [[Upper Council]] as a representative.{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Post-war period == After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the Nasu family lost most of its land holdings due to [[:en:Land reform|Land reform]]. The family relocated to [[:en:Hirosaki|Hirosaki]], [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], seeking aid from their relatives in the [[:en:Tsugaru clan|Tsugaru clan]]. The 36th head, [[Nasu Takashi]], became embroiled in the [[Hirosaki University Professor's Wife Murder Case]], later exonerated after 30 years.{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Nasu Takashi|title=Interview: The Agony of Wrongful Conviction|journal=[[Law Seminar]]|volume=37|issue=1|pages=84-88|year=1992}} In 2004, Takashi entrusted 701 heirlooms, including items linked to [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], to the city of [[:en:Ōtawara|Ōtawara]], helping establish the [[Nasu no Yoichi Denshōkan]] museum.Nasu Yoichi Museum pamphlet, 2004. The 37th head, [[Nasu Masami]], continues to maintain the clan’s legacy. == Key Figures == * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Yoichi) * [[Nasu Sukesada]] (Gorō) * [[Nasu Sukemasa]] (First unified head) * [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]] * [[Nasu Sukekage]] (Founder of Nasu Domain) * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Final daimyō) == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Nasu Seven Cavalry]] * [[Ōtawara clan]] * [[Ōzeki clan]] * [[Nasu Chronicles]] * [[Chisaka clan]] * [[Chisaka Kagechika]] * [[Nasu warriors]] {{translated page|ja|那須氏|version=102902072|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[ja:那須氏]] [[uk:%D0%A0%D1%96%D0%B4 %D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%83]] [[de:Nasu (Klan)]] [[it:Clan Nasu]] [[Category:History of Otawara City]] [[Category:History of Yaita City]] [[Category:Nasu Clan]] [[Category:History of Nasukarasuyama City]] [[Category:Clans of Kantō Region]] [[Category:Samurai Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] {{wikidata link|Q11642934}}

[[de:Nasu (Klan)]][[it:Clan Nasu]][[ja:那須氏]][[uk:Рід Насу]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11642934]] Wikidata name: Nasu clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Clan | name = Nasu clan | crest = 一文字（丸に一文字）紋.png | crest_caption = {{ruby|Ichimonji|いちもんじ}}
{{ruby|Sixteen-petal chrysanthemum with Ichimonji|じゅうろくようぎく に いちもんじ}} | surname = '''Claimed''' to be descendants of [[:en:Fujiwara Hokke|Fujiwara Hokke]], [[Fujiwara no Nagai|Nagai branch]] (possibly descendants of [[Nasu no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko]]) | founder = [[Fujiwara no Sukie]] | type = [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]
[[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] | origin = [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]], [[:en:Nasu District|Nasu District]] | home_province = Shimotsuke Province, Nasu District | notable_members = [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu Suketaka (Nasu no Yoichi)]] | cadet_branches = [[Senbon clan]] (samurai)
[[Fukuhara clan]] (samurai)
[[Iōno clan]] (samurai)
[[Inazawa clan#Nasu Inazawa clan|Inazawa clan]] (samurai) }} The '''Nasu clan''' (那須氏) was a [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] and [[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] family from [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. They originated from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]'s [[:en:Nasu District|Nasu District]] and became widely known for their member [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], who famously shot a fan off a ship during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]] while fighting for [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] in the [[:en:Genpei War|Genpei War]].{{Kotobank|1=Nasu clan|2=World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition}} Following the establishment of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], they unified under the Nasu clan head in [[:en:Karasuyama, Tochigi|Karasuyama]] and led the [[Nasu warriors]] until the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. After being dispossessed during [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s conquest of [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]], they regained limited status following the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], becoming the [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[Nasu Domain]] and later the [[:en:Karasuyama Domain|Karasuyama Domain]] until their confiscation in 1687 (Jōkyō 4).{{Kotobank|1=Nasu Domain|2=Nipponica}} The family retained their name as [[kōtaiyoriai]] (rotational guards) and joined the [[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] Rank after the [[Meiji Restoration]].{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Origins == The Nasu clan claimed descent from the [[:en:Fujiwara Hokke|Fujiwara Hokke]] through [[Fujiwara no Nagai]]'s lineage, with the purported founder being his grandson [[Fujiwara no Sukie|Sukie]] (also known as Sadafumi).{{Efn|Research suggests that Sukie’s father, [[Fujiwara no Michie (Mikosazuke branch)|Fujiwara no Michie]], may have died young, making Sukie’s lineage questionable.Takako Noguchi, "Residential Inheritance in Heian Aristocracy," in {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Takako Noguchi|title=Space and Time of Heian Aristocracy|publisher=Seibundo Shuppan|date=2024|isbn=978-4-7924-1533-4|pages=97-99}}}} Initially called the Sudo clan, they adopted the Nasu name during the era of [[Nasu Suketaka]]."Nasu Genealogy" in {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}} Vol. 149.{{Efn|Some versions of the genealogy, such as "Yamanouchi Sudo Genealogy," attribute their lineage to [[Fujiwara no Tamenori]]'s descendants.}} Although the clan is most famously associated with [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] for his exploits during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]], verifiable historical records begin with [[Nasu Mitsusuke]] in the early [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. Questions surround earlier genealogies and tales, with some theories suggesting descent from the [[Nasu no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko]] family, an ancient local ruling family.Ryo Ota, "Nasu, surname of Fujiwara," in {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Ryo Ota|title=Surname and Family Dictionary|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1942}}. == Overview == After the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] was established, the Nasu clan served as [[:en:Gokenin|Gokenin]] (direct vassals). They reached their zenith during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], being counted among the prestigious [[Kantō Eight Manors]]. However, internal strife in the 15th century divided the clan into the Upper and Lower Nasu families, weakening their position.{{Efn|Evidence suggests this division began earlier, during the [[Nanboku-chō period]].{{Sfn|Eda|2008|p={{page needed|date=2023}}}}}} The Upper Nasu aligned with the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]], while the Lower Nasu supported the [[:en:Kamakura Kubō|Kamakura Kubō]] and later the [[Koga Kubō]]. Despite briefly reunifying under [[Nasu Sukefusa]] in 1514 (Eishō 11), the clan's influence waned due to ongoing conflicts with neighboring [[Utsunomiya clan|Utsunomiya]] and [[:en:Satake clan|Satake clans]]. During [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s [[:en:Odawara Campaign|Odawara Campaign]], [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]]'s delayed arrival led to their dispossession, though his son [[Nasu Sukekage]] retained 5,000 koku under the mediation of [[Ōtawara Harukiyo]]. After siding with the [[Tokugawa clan]] during the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], the clan was granted 14,000 koku and became the daimyō of the Nasu Domain, later moving to the Karasuyama Domain with 20,000 koku. The domain was confiscated in 1687 due to the [[Karasuyama Incident]], leaving the clan with 1,000 koku as kōtaiyoriai under [[Nasu Sukemasa]]. The last head before the [[Meiji Restoration]], [[Nasu Suketaka]], supported the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]], receiving courtier status and participating in the [[Upper Council]] as a representative.{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Post-war period == After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the Nasu family lost most of its land holdings due to [[:en:Land reform|Land reform]]. The family relocated to [[:en:Hirosaki|Hirosaki]], [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], seeking aid from their relatives in the [[:en:Tsugaru clan|Tsugaru clan]]. The 36th head, [[Nasu Takashi]], became embroiled in the [[Hirosaki University Professor's Wife Murder Case]], later exonerated after 30 years.{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Nasu Takashi|title=Interview: The Agony of Wrongful Conviction|journal=[[Law Seminar]]|volume=37|issue=1|pages=84-88|year=1992}} In 2004, Takashi entrusted 701 heirlooms, including items linked to [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], to the city of [[:en:Ōtawara|Ōtawara]], helping establish the [[Nasu no Yoichi Denshōkan]] museum.Nasu Yoichi Museum pamphlet, 2004. The 37th head, [[Nasu Masami]], continues to maintain the clan’s legacy. == Key Figures == * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Yoichi) * [[Nasu Sukesada]] (Gorō) * [[Nasu Sukemasa]] (First unified head) * [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]] * [[Nasu Sukekage]] (Founder of Nasu Domain) * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Final daimyō) == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Nasu Seven Cavalry]] * [[Ōtawara clan]] * [[Ōzeki clan]] * [[Nasu Chronicles]] * [[Chisaka clan]] * [[Chisaka Kagechika]] * [[Nasu warriors]] {{translated page|ja|那須氏|version=102902072|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[ja:那須氏]] [[uk:%D0%A0%D1%96%D0%B4 %D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%83]] [[de:Nasu (Klan)]] [[it:Clan Nasu]] [[Category:History of Otawara City]] [[Category:History of Yaita City]] [[Category:Nasu Clan]] [[Category:History of Nasukarasuyama City]] [[Category:Clans of Kantō Region]] [[Category:Samurai Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] {{wikidata link|Q11642934}}

The 37th head, [[Nasu Masami]], continues to maintain the clan’s legacy. == Key Figures == * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Yoichi) * [[Nasu Sukesada]] (Gorō) * [[Nasu Sukemasa]] (First unified head) * [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]] * [[Nasu Sukekage]] (Founder of Nasu Domain) * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Final daimyō) == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Nasu Seven Cavalry]] * [[Ōtawara clan]] * [[Ōzeki clan]] * [[Nasu Chronicles]] * [[Chisaka clan]] * [[Chisaka Kagechika]] * [[Nasu warriors]] {{translated page|ja|那須氏|version=102902072|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[ja:那須氏]] [[uk:%D0%A0%D1%96%D0%B4 %D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%83]] [[de:Nasu (Klan)]] [[it:Clan Nasu]] [[Category:History of Otawara City]] [[Category:History of Yaita City]] [[Category:Nasu Clan]] [[Category:History of Nasukarasuyama City]] [[Category:Clans of Kantō Region]] [[Category:Samurai Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] {{wikidata link|Q11642934}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[de:Nasu (Klan)]][[it:Clan Nasu]][[ja:那須氏]][[uk:Рід Насу]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11642934]] Wikidata name: Nasu clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Clan | name = Nasu clan | crest = 一文字（丸に一文字）紋.png | crest_caption = {{ruby|Ichimonji|いちもんじ}}
{{ruby|Sixteen-petal chrysanthemum with Ichimonji|じゅうろくようぎく に いちもんじ}} | surname = '''Claimed''' to be descendants of [[:en:Fujiwara Hokke|Fujiwara Hokke]], [[Fujiwara no Nagai|Nagai branch]] (possibly descendants of [[Nasu no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko]]) | founder = [[Fujiwara no Sukie]] | type = [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]
[[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] | origin = [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]], [[:en:Nasu District|Nasu District]] | home_province = Shimotsuke Province, Nasu District | notable_members = [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu Suketaka (Nasu no Yoichi)]] | cadet_branches = [[Senbon clan]] (samurai)
[[Fukuhara clan]] (samurai)
[[Iōno clan]] (samurai)
[[Inazawa clan#Nasu Inazawa clan|Inazawa clan]] (samurai) }} The '''Nasu clan''' (那須氏) was a [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] and [[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] family from [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. They originated from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]'s [[:en:Nasu District|Nasu District]] and became widely known for their member [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], who famously shot a fan off a ship during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]] while fighting for [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] in the [[:en:Genpei War|Genpei War]].{{Kotobank|1=Nasu clan|2=World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition}} Following the establishment of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], they unified under the Nasu clan head in [[:en:Karasuyama, Tochigi|Karasuyama]] and led the [[Nasu warriors]] until the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. After being dispossessed during [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s conquest of [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]], they regained limited status following the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], becoming the [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[Nasu Domain]] and later the [[:en:Karasuyama Domain|Karasuyama Domain]] until their confiscation in 1687 (Jōkyō 4).{{Kotobank|1=Nasu Domain|2=Nipponica}} The family retained their name as [[kōtaiyoriai]] (rotational guards) and joined the [[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] Rank after the [[Meiji Restoration]].{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Origins == The Nasu clan claimed descent from the [[:en:Fujiwara Hokke|Fujiwara Hokke]] through [[Fujiwara no Nagai]]'s lineage, with the purported founder being his grandson [[Fujiwara no Sukie|Sukie]] (also known as Sadafumi).{{Efn|Research suggests that Sukie’s father, [[Fujiwara no Michie (Mikosazuke branch)|Fujiwara no Michie]], may have died young, making Sukie’s lineage questionable.Takako Noguchi, "Residential Inheritance in Heian Aristocracy," in {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Takako Noguchi|title=Space and Time of Heian Aristocracy|publisher=Seibundo Shuppan|date=2024|isbn=978-4-7924-1533-4|pages=97-99}}}} Initially called the Sudo clan, they adopted the Nasu name during the era of [[Nasu Suketaka]]."Nasu Genealogy" in {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}} Vol. 149.{{Efn|Some versions of the genealogy, such as "Yamanouchi Sudo Genealogy," attribute their lineage to [[Fujiwara no Tamenori]]'s descendants.}} Although the clan is most famously associated with [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] for his exploits during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]], verifiable historical records begin with [[Nasu Mitsusuke]] in the early [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. Questions surround earlier genealogies and tales, with some theories suggesting descent from the [[Nasu no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko]] family, an ancient local ruling family.Ryo Ota, "Nasu, surname of Fujiwara," in {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Ryo Ota|title=Surname and Family Dictionary|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1942}}. == Overview == After the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] was established, the Nasu clan served as [[:en:Gokenin|Gokenin]] (direct vassals). They reached their zenith during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], being counted among the prestigious [[Kantō Eight Manors]]. However, internal strife in the 15th century divided the clan into the Upper and Lower Nasu families, weakening their position.{{Efn|Evidence suggests this division began earlier, during the [[Nanboku-chō period]].{{Sfn|Eda|2008|p={{page needed|date=2023}}}}}} The Upper Nasu aligned with the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]], while the Lower Nasu supported the [[:en:Kamakura Kubō|Kamakura Kubō]] and later the [[Koga Kubō]]. Despite briefly reunifying under [[Nasu Sukefusa]] in 1514 (Eishō 11), the clan's influence waned due to ongoing conflicts with neighboring [[Utsunomiya clan|Utsunomiya]] and [[:en:Satake clan|Satake clans]]. During [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s [[:en:Odawara Campaign|Odawara Campaign]], [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]]'s delayed arrival led to their dispossession, though his son [[Nasu Sukekage]] retained 5,000 koku under the mediation of [[Ōtawara Harukiyo]]. After siding with the [[Tokugawa clan]] during the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], the clan was granted 14,000 koku and became the daimyō of the Nasu Domain, later moving to the Karasuyama Domain with 20,000 koku. The domain was confiscated in 1687 due to the [[Karasuyama Incident]], leaving the clan with 1,000 koku as kōtaiyoriai under [[Nasu Sukemasa]]. The last head before the [[Meiji Restoration]], [[Nasu Suketaka]], supported the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]], receiving courtier status and participating in the [[Upper Council]] as a representative.{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Post-war period == After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the Nasu family lost most of its land holdings due to [[:en:Land reform|Land reform]]. The family relocated to [[:en:Hirosaki|Hirosaki]], [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], seeking aid from their relatives in the [[:en:Tsugaru clan|Tsugaru clan]]. The 36th head, [[Nasu Takashi]], became embroiled in the [[Hirosaki University Professor's Wife Murder Case]], later exonerated after 30 years.{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Nasu Takashi|title=Interview: The Agony of Wrongful Conviction|journal=[[Law Seminar]]|volume=37|issue=1|pages=84-88|year=1992}} In 2004, Takashi entrusted 701 heirlooms, including items linked to [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], to the city of [[:en:Ōtawara|Ōtawara]], helping establish the [[Nasu no Yoichi Denshōkan]] museum.Nasu Yoichi Museum pamphlet, 2004. The 37th head, [[Nasu Masami]], continues to maintain the clan’s legacy. == Key Figures == * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Yoichi) * [[Nasu Sukesada]] (Gorō) * [[Nasu Sukemasa]] (First unified head) * [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]] * [[Nasu Sukekage]] (Founder of Nasu Domain) * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Final daimyō) == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Nasu Seven Cavalry]] * [[Ōtawara clan]] * [[Ōzeki clan]] * [[Nasu Chronicles]] * [[Chisaka clan]] * [[Chisaka Kagechika]] * [[Nasu warriors]] {{translated page|ja|那須氏|version=102902072|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[ja:那須氏]] [[uk:%D0%A0%D1%96%D0%B4 %D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%83]] [[de:Nasu (Klan)]] [[it:Clan Nasu]] [[Category:History of Otawara City]] [[Category:History of Yaita City]] [[Category:Nasu Clan]] [[Category:History of Nasukarasuyama City]] [[Category:Clans of Kantō Region]] [[Category:Samurai Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] {{wikidata link|Q11642934}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

During [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s [[:en:Odawara Campaign|Odawara Campaign]], [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]]'s delayed arrival led to their dispossession, though his son [[Nasu Sukekage]] retained 5,000 koku under the mediation of [[Ōtawara Harukiyo]]. After siding with the [[Tokugawa clan]] during the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], the clan was granted 14,000 koku and became the daimyō of the Nasu Domain, later moving to the Karasuyama Domain with 20,000 koku. The domain was confiscated in 1687 due to the [[Karasuyama Incident]], leaving the clan with 1,000 koku as kōtaiyoriai under [[Nasu Sukemasa]]. The last head before the [[Meiji Restoration]], [[Nasu Suketaka]], supported the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]], receiving courtier status and participating in the [[Upper Council]] as a representative.{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Post-war period == After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the Nasu family lost most of its land holdings due to [[:en:Land reform|Land reform]]. The family relocated to [[:en:Hirosaki|Hirosaki]], [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], seeking aid from their relatives in the [[:en:Tsugaru clan|Tsugaru clan]]. The 36th head, [[Nasu Takashi]], became embroiled in the [[Hirosaki University Professor's Wife Murder Case]], later exonerated after 30 years.{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Nasu Takashi|title=Interview: The Agony of Wrongful Conviction|journal=[[Law Seminar]]|volume=37|issue=1|pages=84-88|year=1992}} In 2004, Takashi entrusted 701 heirlooms, including items linked to [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], to the city of [[:en:Ōtawara|Ōtawara]], helping establish the [[Nasu no Yoichi Denshōkan]] museum.Nasu Yoichi Museum pamphlet, 2004. The 37th head, [[Nasu Masami]], continues to maintain the clan’s legacy. == Key Figures == * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Yoichi) * [[Nasu Sukesada]] (Gorō) * [[Nasu Sukemasa]] (First unified head) * [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]] * [[Nasu Sukekage]] (Founder of Nasu Domain) * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Final daimyō) == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Nasu Seven Cavalry]] * [[Ōtawara clan]] * [[Ōzeki clan]] * [[Nasu Chronicles]] * [[Chisaka clan]] * [[Chisaka Kagechika]] * [[Nasu warriors]] {{translated page|ja|那須氏|version=102902072|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[ja:那須氏]] [[uk:%D0%A0%D1%96%D0%B4 %D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%83]] [[de:Nasu (Klan)]] [[it:Clan Nasu]] [[Category:History of Otawara City]] [[Category:History of Yaita City]] [[Category:Nasu Clan]] [[Category:History of Nasukarasuyama City]] [[Category:Clans of Kantō Region]] [[Category:Samurai Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] {{wikidata link|Q11642934}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[de:Nasu (Klan)]][[it:Clan Nasu]][[ja:那須氏]][[uk:Рід Насу]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11642934]] Wikidata name: Nasu clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Clan | name = Nasu clan | crest = 一文字（丸に一文字）紋.png | crest_caption = {{ruby|Ichimonji|いちもんじ}}
{{ruby|Sixteen-petal chrysanthemum with Ichimonji|じゅうろくようぎく に いちもんじ}} | surname = '''Claimed''' to be descendants of [[:en:Fujiwara Hokke|Fujiwara Hokke]], [[Fujiwara no Nagai|Nagai branch]] (possibly descendants of [[Nasu no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko]]) | founder = [[Fujiwara no Sukie]] | type = [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]
[[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] | origin = [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]], [[:en:Nasu District|Nasu District]] | home_province = Shimotsuke Province, Nasu District | notable_members = [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu Suketaka (Nasu no Yoichi)]] | cadet_branches = [[Senbon clan]] (samurai)
[[Fukuhara clan]] (samurai)
[[Iōno clan]] (samurai)
[[Inazawa clan#Nasu Inazawa clan|Inazawa clan]] (samurai) }} The '''Nasu clan''' (那須氏) was a [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] and [[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] family from [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. They originated from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]'s [[:en:Nasu District|Nasu District]] and became widely known for their member [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], who famously shot a fan off a ship during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]] while fighting for [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] in the [[:en:Genpei War|Genpei War]].{{Kotobank|1=Nasu clan|2=World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition}} Following the establishment of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], they unified under the Nasu clan head in [[:en:Karasuyama, Tochigi|Karasuyama]] and led the [[Nasu warriors]] until the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. After being dispossessed during [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s conquest of [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]], they regained limited status following the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], becoming the [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[Nasu Domain]] and later the [[:en:Karasuyama Domain|Karasuyama Domain]] until their confiscation in 1687 (Jōkyō 4).{{Kotobank|1=Nasu Domain|2=Nipponica}} The family retained their name as [[kōtaiyoriai]] (rotational guards) and joined the [[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] Rank after the [[Meiji Restoration]].{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Origins == The Nasu clan claimed descent from the [[:en:Fujiwara Hokke|Fujiwara Hokke]] through [[Fujiwara no Nagai]]'s lineage, with the purported founder being his grandson [[Fujiwara no Sukie|Sukie]] (also known as Sadafumi).{{Efn|Research suggests that Sukie’s father, [[Fujiwara no Michie (Mikosazuke branch)|Fujiwara no Michie]], may have died young, making Sukie’s lineage questionable.Takako Noguchi, "Residential Inheritance in Heian Aristocracy," in {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Takako Noguchi|title=Space and Time of Heian Aristocracy|publisher=Seibundo Shuppan|date=2024|isbn=978-4-7924-1533-4|pages=97-99}}}} Initially called the Sudo clan, they adopted the Nasu name during the era of [[Nasu Suketaka]]."Nasu Genealogy" in {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}} Vol. 149.{{Efn|Some versions of the genealogy, such as "Yamanouchi Sudo Genealogy," attribute their lineage to [[Fujiwara no Tamenori]]'s descendants.}} Although the clan is most famously associated with [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] for his exploits during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]], verifiable historical records begin with [[Nasu Mitsusuke]] in the early [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. Questions surround earlier genealogies and tales, with some theories suggesting descent from the [[Nasu no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko]] family, an ancient local ruling family.Ryo Ota, "Nasu, surname of Fujiwara," in {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Ryo Ota|title=Surname and Family Dictionary|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1942}}. == Overview == After the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] was established, the Nasu clan served as [[:en:Gokenin|Gokenin]] (direct vassals). They reached their zenith during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], being counted among the prestigious [[Kantō Eight Manors]]. However, internal strife in the 15th century divided the clan into the Upper and Lower Nasu families, weakening their position.{{Efn|Evidence suggests this division began earlier, during the [[Nanboku-chō period]].{{Sfn|Eda|2008|p={{page needed|date=2023}}}}}} The Upper Nasu aligned with the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]], while the Lower Nasu supported the [[:en:Kamakura Kubō|Kamakura Kubō]] and later the [[Koga Kubō]]. Despite briefly reunifying under [[Nasu Sukefusa]] in 1514 (Eishō 11), the clan's influence waned due to ongoing conflicts with neighboring [[Utsunomiya clan|Utsunomiya]] and [[:en:Satake clan|Satake clans]]. During [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s [[:en:Odawara Campaign|Odawara Campaign]], [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]]'s delayed arrival led to their dispossession, though his son [[Nasu Sukekage]] retained 5,000 koku under the mediation of [[Ōtawara Harukiyo]]. After siding with the [[Tokugawa clan]] during the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], the clan was granted 14,000 koku and became the daimyō of the Nasu Domain, later moving to the Karasuyama Domain with 20,000 koku. The domain was confiscated in 1687 due to the [[Karasuyama Incident]], leaving the clan with 1,000 koku as kōtaiyoriai under [[Nasu Sukemasa]]. The last head before the [[Meiji Restoration]], [[Nasu Suketaka]], supported the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]], receiving courtier status and participating in the [[Upper Council]] as a representative.{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Post-war period == After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the Nasu family lost most of its land holdings due to [[:en:Land reform|Land reform]]. The family relocated to [[:en:Hirosaki|Hirosaki]], [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], seeking aid from their relatives in the [[:en:Tsugaru clan|Tsugaru clan]]. The 36th head, [[Nasu Takashi]], became embroiled in the [[Hirosaki University Professor's Wife Murder Case]], later exonerated after 30 years.{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Nasu Takashi|title=Interview: The Agony of Wrongful Conviction|journal=[[Law Seminar]]|volume=37|issue=1|pages=84-88|year=1992}} In 2004, Takashi entrusted 701 heirlooms, including items linked to [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], to the city of [[:en:Ōtawara|Ōtawara]], helping establish the [[Nasu no Yoichi Denshōkan]] museum.Nasu Yoichi Museum pamphlet, 2004. The 37th head, [[Nasu Masami]], continues to maintain the clan’s legacy. == Key Figures == * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Yoichi) * [[Nasu Sukesada]] (Gorō) * [[Nasu Sukemasa]] (First unified head) * [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]] * [[Nasu Sukekage]] (Founder of Nasu Domain) * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Final daimyō) == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Nasu Seven Cavalry]] * [[Ōtawara clan]] * [[Ōzeki clan]] * [[Nasu Chronicles]] * [[Chisaka clan]] * [[Chisaka Kagechika]] * [[Nasu warriors]] {{translated page|ja|那須氏|version=102902072|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[ja:那須氏]] [[uk:%D0%A0%D1%96%D0%B4 %D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%83]] [[de:Nasu (Klan)]] [[it:Clan Nasu]] [[Category:History of Otawara City]] [[Category:History of Yaita City]] [[Category:Nasu Clan]] [[Category:History of Nasukarasuyama City]] [[Category:Clans of Kantō Region]] [[Category:Samurai Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] {{wikidata link|Q11642934}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

They originated from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]'s [[:en:Nasu District|Nasu District]] and became widely known for their member [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], who famously shot a fan off a ship during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]] while fighting for [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] in the [[:en:Genpei War|Genpei War]].{{Kotobank|1=Nasu clan|2=World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition}} Following the establishment of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], they unified under the Nasu clan head in [[:en:Karasuyama, Tochigi|Karasuyama]] and led the [[Nasu warriors]] until the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. After being dispossessed during [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s conquest of [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]], they regained limited status following the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], becoming the [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[Nasu Domain]] and later the [[:en:Karasuyama Domain|Karasuyama Domain]] until their confiscation in 1687 (Jōkyō 4).{{Kotobank|1=Nasu Domain|2=Nipponica}} The family retained their name as [[kōtaiyoriai]] (rotational guards) and joined the [[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] Rank after the [[Meiji Restoration]].{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Origins == The Nasu clan claimed descent from the [[:en:Fujiwara Hokke|Fujiwara Hokke]] through [[Fujiwara no Nagai]]'s lineage, with the purported founder being his grandson [[Fujiwara no Sukie|Sukie]] (also known as Sadafumi).{{Efn|Research suggests that Sukie’s father, [[Fujiwara no Michie (Mikosazuke branch)|Fujiwara no Michie]], may have died young, making Sukie’s lineage questionable.Takako Noguchi, "Residential Inheritance in Heian Aristocracy," in {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Takako Noguchi|title=Space and Time of Heian Aristocracy|publisher=Seibundo Shuppan|date=2024|isbn=978-4-7924-1533-4|pages=97-99}}}} Initially called the Sudo clan, they adopted the Nasu name during the era of [[Nasu Suketaka]]."Nasu Genealogy" in {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}} Vol. 149.{{Efn|Some versions of the genealogy, such as "Yamanouchi Sudo Genealogy," attribute their lineage to [[Fujiwara no Tamenori]]'s descendants.}} Although the clan is most famously associated with [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] for his exploits during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]], verifiable historical records begin with [[Nasu Mitsusuke]] in the early [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. Questions surround earlier genealogies and tales, with some theories suggesting descent from the [[Nasu no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko]] family, an ancient local ruling family.Ryo Ota, "Nasu, surname of Fujiwara," in {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Ryo Ota|title=Surname and Family Dictionary|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1942}}. == Overview == After the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] was established, the Nasu clan served as [[:en:Gokenin|Gokenin]] (direct vassals). They reached their zenith during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], being counted among the prestigious [[Kantō Eight Manors]]. However, internal strife in the 15th century divided the clan into the Upper and Lower Nasu families, weakening their position.{{Efn|Evidence suggests this division began earlier, during the [[Nanboku-chō period]].{{Sfn|Eda|2008|p={{page needed|date=2023}}}}}} The Upper Nasu aligned with the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]], while the Lower Nasu supported the [[:en:Kamakura Kubō|Kamakura Kubō]] and later the [[Koga Kubō]]. Despite briefly reunifying under [[Nasu Sukefusa]] in 1514 (Eishō 11), the clan's influence waned due to ongoing conflicts with neighboring [[Utsunomiya clan|Utsunomiya]] and [[:en:Satake clan|Satake clans]]. During [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s [[:en:Odawara Campaign|Odawara Campaign]], [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]]'s delayed arrival led to their dispossession, though his son [[Nasu Sukekage]] retained 5,000 koku under the mediation of [[Ōtawara Harukiyo]]. After siding with the [[Tokugawa clan]] during the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], the clan was granted 14,000 koku and became the daimyō of the Nasu Domain, later moving to the Karasuyama Domain with 20,000 koku. The domain was confiscated in 1687 due to the [[Karasuyama Incident]], leaving the clan with 1,000 koku as kōtaiyoriai under [[Nasu Sukemasa]]. The last head before the [[Meiji Restoration]], [[Nasu Suketaka]], supported the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]], receiving courtier status and participating in the [[Upper Council]] as a representative.{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Post-war period == After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the Nasu family lost most of its land holdings due to [[:en:Land reform|Land reform]]. The family relocated to [[:en:Hirosaki|Hirosaki]], [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], seeking aid from their relatives in the [[:en:Tsugaru clan|Tsugaru clan]]. The 36th head, [[Nasu Takashi]], became embroiled in the [[Hirosaki University Professor's Wife Murder Case]], later exonerated after 30 years.{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Nasu Takashi|title=Interview: The Agony of Wrongful Conviction|journal=[[Law Seminar]]|volume=37|issue=1|pages=84-88|year=1992}} In 2004, Takashi entrusted 701 heirlooms, including items linked to [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], to the city of [[:en:Ōtawara|Ōtawara]], helping establish the [[Nasu no Yoichi Denshōkan]] museum.Nasu Yoichi Museum pamphlet, 2004. The 37th head, [[Nasu Masami]], continues to maintain the clan’s legacy. == Key Figures == * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Yoichi) * [[Nasu Sukesada]] (Gorō) * [[Nasu Sukemasa]] (First unified head) * [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]] * [[Nasu Sukekage]] (Founder of Nasu Domain) * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Final daimyō) == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Nasu Seven Cavalry]] * [[Ōtawara clan]] * [[Ōzeki clan]] * [[Nasu Chronicles]] * [[Chisaka clan]] * [[Chisaka Kagechika]] * [[Nasu warriors]] {{translated page|ja|那須氏|version=102902072|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[ja:那須氏]] [[uk:%D0%A0%D1%96%D0%B4 %D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%83]] [[de:Nasu (Klan)]] [[it:Clan Nasu]] [[Category:History of Otawara City]] [[Category:History of Yaita City]] [[Category:Nasu Clan]] [[Category:History of Nasukarasuyama City]] [[Category:Clans of Kantō Region]] [[Category:Samurai Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] {{wikidata link|Q11642934}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

The 36th head, [[Nasu Takashi]], became embroiled in the [[Hirosaki University Professor's Wife Murder Case]], later exonerated after 30 years.{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Nasu Takashi|title=Interview: The Agony of Wrongful Conviction|journal=[[Law Seminar]]|volume=37|issue=1|pages=84-88|year=1992}} In 2004, Takashi entrusted 701 heirlooms, including items linked to [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], to the city of [[:en:Ōtawara|Ōtawara]], helping establish the [[Nasu no Yoichi Denshōkan]] museum.Nasu Yoichi Museum pamphlet, 2004. The 37th head, [[Nasu Masami]], continues to maintain the clan’s legacy. == Key Figures == * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Yoichi) * [[Nasu Sukesada]] (Gorō) * [[Nasu Sukemasa]] (First unified head) * [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]] * [[Nasu Sukekage]] (Founder of Nasu Domain) * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Final daimyō) == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Nasu Seven Cavalry]] * [[Ōtawara clan]] * [[Ōzeki clan]] * [[Nasu Chronicles]] * [[Chisaka clan]] * [[Chisaka Kagechika]] * [[Nasu warriors]] {{translated page|ja|那須氏|version=102902072|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:那須氏]] [[uk:%D0%A0%D1%96%D0%B4 %D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%83]] [[de:Nasu (Klan)]] [[it:Clan Nasu]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] {{wikidata link|Q11642934}} [[Category:Clans of Kantō Region]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:History of Nasukarasuyama City]] [[Category:History of Otawara City]] [[Category:History of Yaita City]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Nasu Clan]] [[Category:Samurai Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

[[de:Nasu (Klan)]][[it:Clan Nasu]][[ja:那須氏]][[uk:Рід Насу]] [[de:Nasu (Klan)]][[it:Clan Nasu]][[ja:那須氏]][[uk:Рід Насу]] [[de:Nasu (Klan)]][[it:Clan Nasu]][[ja:那須氏]][[uk:Рід Насу]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11642934]] Wikidata name: Nasu clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Clan | name = Nasu clan | crest = 一文字（丸に一文字）紋.png | crest_caption = {{ruby|Ichimonji|いちもんじ}}
{{ruby|Sixteen-petal chrysanthemum with Ichimonji|じゅうろくようぎく に いちもんじ}} | surname = '''Claimed''' to be descendants of [[:en:Fujiwara Hokke|Fujiwara Hokke]], [[Fujiwara no Nagai|Nagai branch]] (possibly descendants of [[Nasu no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko]]) | founder = [[Fujiwara no Sukie]] | type = [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]
[[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] | origin = [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]], [[:en:Nasu District|Nasu District]] | home_province = Shimotsuke Province, Nasu District | notable_members = [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu Suketaka (Nasu no Yoichi)]] | cadet_branches = [[Senbon clan]] (samurai)

[[Inazawa clan#Nasu Inazawa clan|Inazawa clan]] (samurai) }} The '''Nasu clan''' (那須氏) was a [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] and [[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] family from [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. They originated from [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]'s [[:en:Nasu District|Nasu District]] and became widely known for their member [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], who famously shot a fan off a ship during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]] while fighting for [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] in the [[:en:Genpei War|Genpei War]].{{Kotobank|1=Nasu clan|2=World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition}} Following the establishment of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], they unified under the Nasu clan head in [[:en:Karasuyama, Tochigi|Karasuyama]] and led the [[Nasu warriors]] until the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]]. After being dispossessed during [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s conquest of [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]], they regained limited status following the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], becoming the [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[Nasu Domain]] and later the [[:en:Karasuyama Domain|Karasuyama Domain]] until their confiscation in 1687 (Jōkyō 4).{{Kotobank|1=Nasu Domain|2=Nipponica}} The family retained their name as [[kōtaiyoriai]] (rotational guards) and joined the [[:en:Shizoku|Shizoku]] Rank after the [[Meiji Restoration]].{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Origins == The Nasu clan claimed descent from the [[:en:Fujiwara Hokke|Fujiwara Hokke]] through [[Fujiwara no Nagai]]'s lineage, with the purported founder being his grandson [[Fujiwara no Sukie|Sukie]] (also known as Sadafumi).{{Efn|Research suggests that Sukie’s father, [[Fujiwara no Michie (Mikosazuke branch)|Fujiwara no Michie]], may have died young, making Sukie’s lineage questionable.Takako Noguchi, "Residential Inheritance in Heian Aristocracy," in {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Takako Noguchi|title=Space and Time of Heian Aristocracy|publisher=Seibundo Shuppan|date=2024|isbn=978-4-7924-1533-4|pages=97-99}}}} Initially called the Sudo clan, they adopted the Nasu name during the era of [[Nasu Suketaka]]."Nasu Genealogy" in {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}} Vol. 149.{{Efn|Some versions of the genealogy, such as "Yamanouchi Sudo Genealogy," attribute their lineage to [[Fujiwara no Tamenori]]'s descendants.}} Although the clan is most famously associated with [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] for his exploits during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]], verifiable historical records begin with [[Nasu Mitsusuke]] in the early [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. Questions surround earlier genealogies and tales, with some theories suggesting descent from the [[Nasu no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko]] family, an ancient local ruling family.Ryo Ota, "Nasu, surname of Fujiwara," in {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Ryo Ota|title=Surname and Family Dictionary|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1942}}. == Overview == After the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] was established, the Nasu clan served as [[:en:Gokenin|Gokenin]] (direct vassals). They reached their zenith during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], being counted among the prestigious [[Kantō Eight Manors]]. However, internal strife in the 15th century divided the clan into the Upper and Lower Nasu families, weakening their position.{{Efn|Evidence suggests this division began earlier, during the [[Nanboku-chō period]].{{Sfn|Eda|2008|p={{page needed|date=2023}}}}}} The Upper Nasu aligned with the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]], while the Lower Nasu supported the [[:en:Kamakura Kubō|Kamakura Kubō]] and later the [[Koga Kubō]]. Despite briefly reunifying under [[Nasu Sukefusa]] in 1514 (Eishō 11), the clan's influence waned due to ongoing conflicts with neighboring [[Utsunomiya clan|Utsunomiya]] and [[:en:Satake clan|Satake clans]]. During [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s [[:en:Odawara Campaign|Odawara Campaign]], [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]]'s delayed arrival led to their dispossession, though his son [[Nasu Sukekage]] retained 5,000 koku under the mediation of [[Ōtawara Harukiyo]]. After siding with the [[Tokugawa clan]] during the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], the clan was granted 14,000 koku and became the daimyō of the Nasu Domain, later moving to the Karasuyama Domain with 20,000 koku. The domain was confiscated in 1687 due to the [[Karasuyama Incident]], leaving the clan with 1,000 koku as kōtaiyoriai under [[Nasu Sukemasa]]. The last head before the [[Meiji Restoration]], [[Nasu Suketaka]], supported the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]], receiving courtier status and participating in the [[Upper Council]] as a representative.{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Post-war period == After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the Nasu family lost most of its land holdings due to [[:en:Land reform|Land reform]]. The family relocated to [[:en:Hirosaki|Hirosaki]], [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], seeking aid from their relatives in the [[:en:Tsugaru clan|Tsugaru clan]]. The 36th head, [[Nasu Takashi]], became embroiled in the [[Hirosaki University Professor's Wife Murder Case]], later exonerated after 30 years.{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Nasu Takashi|title=Interview: The Agony of Wrongful Conviction|journal=[[Law Seminar]]|volume=37|issue=1|pages=84-88|year=1992}} In 2004, Takashi entrusted 701 heirlooms, including items linked to [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], to the city of [[:en:Ōtawara|Ōtawara]], helping establish the [[Nasu no Yoichi Denshōkan]] museum.Nasu Yoichi Museum pamphlet, 2004. The 37th head, [[Nasu Masami]], continues to maintain the clan’s legacy. == Key Figures == * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Yoichi) * [[Nasu Sukesada]] (Gorō) * [[Nasu Sukemasa]] (First unified head) * [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]] * [[Nasu Sukekage]] (Founder of Nasu Domain) * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Final daimyō) == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Nasu Seven Cavalry]] * [[Ōtawara clan]] * [[Ōzeki clan]] * [[Nasu Chronicles]] * [[Chisaka clan]] * [[Chisaka Kagechika]] * [[Nasu warriors]] {{translated page|ja|那須氏|version=102902072|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:那須氏]] [[uk:%D0%A0%D1%96%D0%B4 %D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%83]] [[de:Nasu (Klan)]] [[it:Clan Nasu]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] {{wikidata link|Q11642934}} [[Category:Clans of Kantō Region]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:History of Nasukarasuyama City]] [[Category:History of Otawara City]] [[Category:History of Yaita City]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Nasu Clan]] [[Category:Samurai Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

However, internal strife in the 15th century divided the clan into the Upper and Lower Nasu families, weakening their position.{{Efn|Evidence suggests this division began earlier, during the [[Nanboku-chō period]].{{Sfn|Eda|2008|p={{page needed|date=2023}}}}}} The Upper Nasu aligned with the [[:en:Ashikaga shogunate|Ashikaga shogunate]], while the Lower Nasu supported the [[:en:Kamakura Kubō|Kamakura Kubō]] and later the [[Koga Kubō]]. Despite briefly reunifying under [[Nasu Sukefusa]] in 1514 (Eishō 11), the clan's influence waned due to ongoing conflicts with neighboring [[Utsunomiya clan|Utsunomiya]] and [[:en:Satake clan|Satake clans]]. During [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s [[:en:Odawara Campaign|Odawara Campaign]], [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]]'s delayed arrival led to their dispossession, though his son [[Nasu Sukekage]] retained 5,000 koku under the mediation of [[Ōtawara Harukiyo]]. After siding with the [[Tokugawa clan]] during the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]], the clan was granted 14,000 koku and became the daimyō of the Nasu Domain, later moving to the Karasuyama Domain with 20,000 koku. The domain was confiscated in 1687 due to the [[Karasuyama Incident]], leaving the clan with 1,000 koku as kōtaiyoriai under [[Nasu Sukemasa]]. The last head before the [[Meiji Restoration]], [[Nasu Suketaka]], supported the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]], receiving courtier status and participating in the [[Upper Council]] as a representative.{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Post-war period == After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the Nasu family lost most of its land holdings due to [[:en:Land reform|Land reform]]. The family relocated to [[:en:Hirosaki|Hirosaki]], [[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]], seeking aid from their relatives in the [[:en:Tsugaru clan|Tsugaru clan]]. The 36th head, [[Nasu Takashi]], became embroiled in the [[Hirosaki University Professor's Wife Murder Case]], later exonerated after 30 years.{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Nasu Takashi|title=Interview: The Agony of Wrongful Conviction|journal=[[Law Seminar]]|volume=37|issue=1|pages=84-88|year=1992}} In 2004, Takashi entrusted 701 heirlooms, including items linked to [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]], to the city of [[:en:Ōtawara|Ōtawara]], helping establish the [[Nasu no Yoichi Denshōkan]] museum.Nasu Yoichi Museum pamphlet, 2004. The 37th head, [[Nasu Masami]], continues to maintain the clan’s legacy. == Key Figures == * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Yoichi) * [[Nasu Sukesada]] (Gorō) * [[Nasu Sukemasa]] (First unified head) * [[:en:Nasu Sukeharu|Nasu Sukeharu]] * [[Nasu Sukekage]] (Founder of Nasu Domain) * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Final daimyō) == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Nasu Seven Cavalry]] * [[Ōtawara clan]] * [[Ōzeki clan]] * [[Nasu Chronicles]] * [[Chisaka clan]] * [[Chisaka Kagechika]] * [[Nasu warriors]] {{translated page|ja|那須氏|version=102902072|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:那須氏]] [[uk:%D0%A0%D1%96%D0%B4 %D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%83]] [[de:Nasu (Klan)]] [[it:Clan Nasu]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] {{wikidata link|Q11642934}} [[Category:Clans of Kantō Region]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:History of Nasukarasuyama City]] [[Category:History of Otawara City]] [[Category:History of Yaita City]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Nasu Clan]] [[Category:Samurai Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

[[de:Nasu (Klan)]][[it:Clan Nasu]][[ja:那須氏]][[uk:Рід Насу]] [[de:Nasu (Klan)]][[it:Clan Nasu]][[ja:那須氏]][[uk:Рід Насу]] [[de:Nasu (Klan)]][[it:Clan Nasu]][[ja:那須氏]][[uk:Рід Насу]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11642934]] Wikidata name: Nasu clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Clan | name = Nasu clan | crest = 一文字（丸に一文字）紋.png | crest_caption = {{ruby|Ichimonji|いちもんじ}}
{{ruby|Sixteen-petal chrysanthemum with Ichimonji|じゅうろくようぎく に いちもんじ}} | surname = '''Claimed''' to be descendants of [[:en:Fujiwara Hokke|Fujiwara Hokke]], [[Fujiwara no Nagai|Nagai branch]] (possibly descendants of [[Nasu no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko]]) | founder = [[Fujiwara no Sukie]] | type = {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}}
{{ill|Shizoku|en|Shizoku|WD=Q3482406|lt=Shizoku}} | origin = {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|en|Shimotsuke Province|WD=Q1053699|lt=Shimotsuke Province}}, [[:en:Nasu District|Nasu District]] | home_province = Shimotsuke Province, Nasu District | notable_members = {{ill|Nasu no Yoichi|en|Nasu no Yoichi|WD=Q2625800|lt=Nasu Suketaka (Nasu no Yoichi)}} | cadet_branches = [[Senbon clan]] (samurai)
[[Fukuhara clan]] (samurai)
[[Iōno clan]] (samurai)
[[Inazawa clan#Nasu Inazawa clan|Inazawa clan]] (samurai) }} The '''Nasu clan''' (那須氏) was a {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} and {{ill|Shizoku|en|Shizoku|WD=Q3482406|lt=Shizoku}} family from {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. They originated from {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|en|Shimotsuke Province|WD=Q1053699|lt=Shimotsuke Province}}'s [[:en:Nasu District|Nasu District]] and became widely known for their member {{ill|Nasu no Yoichi|en|Nasu no Yoichi|WD=Q2625800|lt=Nasu no Yoichi}}, who famously shot a fan off a ship during the {{ill|Battle of Yashima|en|Battle of Yashima|WD=Q2117322|lt=Battle of Yashima}} while fighting for [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] in the {{ill|Genpei War|en|Genpei War|WD=Q737542|lt=Genpei War}}.{{Kotobank|1=Nasu clan|2=World Encyclopedia, 2nd Edition}} Following the establishment of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, they unified under the Nasu clan head in {{ill|Karasuyama, Tochigi|en|Karasuyama, Tochigi|WD=Q6368658|lt=Karasuyama}} and led the [[Nasu warriors]] until the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}. After being dispossessed during {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s conquest of [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]], they regained limited status following the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}}, becoming the [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[Nasu Domain]] and later the {{ill|Karasuyama Domain|en|Karasuyama Domain|WD=Q10863343|lt=Karasuyama Domain}} until their confiscation in 1687 (Jōkyō 4).{{Kotobank|1=Nasu Domain|2=Nipponica}} The family retained their name as [[kōtaiyoriai]] (rotational guards) and joined the {{ill|Shizoku|en|Shizoku|WD=Q3482406|lt=Shizoku}} Rank after the [[Meiji Restoration]].{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Origins == The Nasu clan claimed descent from the [[:en:Fujiwara Hokke|Fujiwara Hokke]] through [[Fujiwara no Nagai]]'s lineage, with the purported founder being his grandson [[Fujiwara no Sukie|Sukie]] (also known as Sadafumi).{{Efn|Research suggests that Sukie’s father, [[Fujiwara no Michie (Mikosazuke branch)|Fujiwara no Michie]], may have died young, making Sukie’s lineage questionable.Takako Noguchi, "Residential Inheritance in Heian Aristocracy," in {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Takako Noguchi|title=Space and Time of Heian Aristocracy|publisher=Seibundo Shuppan|date=2024|isbn=978-4-7924-1533-4|pages=97-99}}}} Initially called the Sudo clan, they adopted the Nasu name during the era of [[Nasu Suketaka]]."Nasu Genealogy" in {{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従|1=Gunsho Ruijū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1554852}} Vol. 149.{{Efn|Some versions of the genealogy, such as "Yamanouchi Sudo Genealogy," attribute their lineage to [[Fujiwara no Tamenori]]'s descendants.}} Although the clan is most famously associated with {{ill|Nasu no Yoichi|en|Nasu no Yoichi|WD=Q2625800|lt=Nasu no Yoichi}} for his exploits during the {{ill|Battle of Yashima|en|Battle of Yashima|WD=Q2117322|lt=Battle of Yashima}}, verifiable historical records begin with [[Nasu Mitsusuke]] in the early {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}. Questions surround earlier genealogies and tales, with some theories suggesting descent from the [[Nasu no Kuni-no-Miyatsuko]] family, an ancient local ruling family.Ryo Ota, "Nasu, surname of Fujiwara," in {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Ryo Ota|title=Surname and Family Dictionary|publisher=Kokuminsha|year=1942}}. == Overview == After the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}} was established, the Nasu clan served as {{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|WD=Q63765|lt=Gokenin}} (direct vassals). They reached their zenith during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, being counted among the prestigious [[Kantō Eight Manors]]. However, internal strife in the 15th century divided the clan into the Upper and Lower Nasu families, weakening their position.{{Efn|Evidence suggests this division began earlier, during the [[Nanboku-chō period]].{{Sfn|Eda|2008|p={{page needed|date=2023}}}}}} The Upper Nasu aligned with the {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga shogunate}}, while the Lower Nasu supported the [[:en:Kamakura Kubō|Kamakura Kubō]] and later the [[Koga Kubō]]. Despite briefly reunifying under [[Nasu Sukefusa]] in 1514 (Eishō 11), the clan's influence waned due to ongoing conflicts with neighboring [[Utsunomiya clan|Utsunomiya]] and {{ill|Satake clan|en|Satake clan|WD=Q2975363|lt=Satake clans}}. During {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s [[:en:Odawara Campaign|Odawara Campaign]], {{ill|Nasu Sukeharu|en|Nasu Sukeharu|WD=Q6967760|lt=Nasu Sukeharu}}'s delayed arrival led to their dispossession, though his son [[Nasu Sukekage]] retained 5,000 koku under the mediation of [[Ōtawara Harukiyo]]. After siding with the [[Tokugawa clan]] during the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}}, the clan was granted 14,000 koku and became the daimyō of the Nasu Domain, later moving to the Karasuyama Domain with 20,000 koku. The domain was confiscated in 1687 due to the [[Karasuyama Incident]], leaving the clan with 1,000 koku as kōtaiyoriai under [[Nasu Sukemasa]]. The last head before the [[Meiji Restoration]], [[Nasu Suketaka]], supported the {{ill|Imperial Court|en|Imperial Court|WD=Q6006523|lt=Imperial Court}}, receiving courtier status and participating in the [[Upper Council]] as a representative.{{sfn|Matsuda Keiji|2015|p=524}} == Post-war period == After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, the Nasu family lost most of its land holdings due to {{ill|Land reform|en|Land reform|WD=Q208128|lt=Land reform}}. The family relocated to {{ill|Hirosaki|en|Hirosaki|WD=Q194087|lt=Hirosaki}}, {{ill|Aomori Prefecture|en|Aomori Prefecture|WD=Q71699|lt=Aomori Prefecture}}, seeking aid from their relatives in the {{ill|Tsugaru clan|en|Tsugaru clan|WD=Q1062437|lt=Tsugaru clan}}. The 36th head, [[Nasu Takashi]], became embroiled in the [[Hirosaki University Professor's Wife Murder Case]], later exonerated after 30 years.{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Nasu Takashi|title=Interview: The Agony of Wrongful Conviction|journal=[[Law Seminar]]|volume=37|issue=1|pages=84-88|year=1992}} In 2004, Takashi entrusted 701 heirlooms, including items linked to {{ill|Nasu no Yoichi|en|Nasu no Yoichi|WD=Q2625800|lt=Nasu no Yoichi}}, to the city of {{ill|Ōtawara|en|Ōtawara|WD=Q386322|lt=Ōtawara}}, helping establish the [[Nasu no Yoichi Denshōkan]] museum.Nasu Yoichi Museum pamphlet, 2004. The 37th head, [[Nasu Masami]], continues to maintain the clan’s legacy. == Key Figures == * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Yoichi) * [[Nasu Sukesada]] (Gorō) * [[Nasu Sukemasa]] (First unified head) * {{ill|Nasu Sukeharu|en|Nasu Sukeharu|WD=Q6967760|lt=Nasu Sukeharu}} * [[Nasu Sukekage]] (Founder of Nasu Domain) * [[Nasu Suketaka]] (Final daimyō) == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[Nasu Seven Cavalry]] * [[Ōtawara clan]] * [[Ōzeki clan]] * [[Nasu Chronicles]] * [[Chisaka clan]] * [[Chisaka Kagechika]] * [[Nasu warriors]] {{translated page|ja|那須氏|version=102902072|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:那須氏]] [[uk:%D0%A0%D1%96%D0%B4 %D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%83]] [[de:Nasu (Klan)]] [[it:Clan Nasu]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] [[uk:Рід Насу]] {{wikidata link|Q11642934}} [[Category:Clans of Kantō Region]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:History of Nasukarasuyama City]] [[Category:History of Otawara City]] [[Category:History of Yaita City]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Nasu Clan]] [[Category:Samurai Clans of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

The 36th head, [[Nasu Takashi]], became embroiled in the [[Hirosaki University Professor's Wife Murder Case]], later exonerated after 30 years.{{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Nasu Takashi|title=Interview: The Agony of Wrongful Conviction|journal=[[Law Seminar]]|volume=37|issue=1|pages=84-88|year=1992}} In 2004, Takashi entrusted 701 heirlooms, including items linked to {{ill|Nasu no Yoichi|en|Nasu no Yoichi|WD=Q2625800|lt=Nasu no Yoichi}}, to the city of {{ill|Ōtawara|en|Ōtawara|WD=Q386322|lt=Ōtawara}}, helping establish the [[Nasu no Yoichi Denshōkan]] museum.Nasu Yoichi Museum pamphlet, 2004.
